data.title: Megan Thee Stallion hospitalized after leaving 'Moulin Rouge' performance mid-show
data.description:
MANHATTAN, N.Y. (WPIX) – Rapper Megan Thee Stallion’s Broadway performance in “Moulin Rouge! The Musical” was cut short on Tuesday night as she was rushed to a hospital.

The Grammy Award-winning rapper made her Broadway debut last week as nightclub impresario Harold Zidler in the show at the Al Hirschfeld Theatre, and was expected to conclude her run on May 17.

The 31-year-old rap star shared a video on her social media last week, announcing her debut.

Radio personality Loren Lorosa was among the audience at the Hirschfeld Theatre on Tuesday, and posted on social media that Megan performed well during her opening scenes. Then, midway through the show, an announcement was made, Lorosa said.

“They just stopped Moulin Rouge mid-show apologized to the audience and said … ‘Stay inside and seated,'” Lorosa explained, adding that the show eventually continued with another actor stepping in for Megan.

The rapper’s team has so far revealed that she fell “very ill.”

“During Tuesday night’s production, Megan started feeling very ill and was promptly transported to a local hospital, where her symptoms are currently being evaluated,” her representative, Didier Morais, said in a statement. “We will share additional updates as more information becomes available.”

Megan has been very vocal about her excitement for her “Moulin Rouge” role, which she was set to play through mid-May. The hip-hop star, known for hits like “Savage” and “WAP” with Cardi B, was apparently off to a strong start, claiming that her appearance boosted overnight ticket sales.

“Moulin Rouge! The Musical” is slated to close July 26 after a seven-year run. The show is about the goings-on in a turn-of-the-century Parisian nightclub, updated with tunes like “Single Ladies” and “Firework” alongside “Lady Marmalade.”

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

data.title: UK's Starmer defends NATO after Trump criticism
data.description:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer defended NATO on Wednesday after President Trump suggested that the U.S. would reconsider its membership to the longstanding alliance once the conflict with Iran concludes.

“NATO is the single most effective military alliance the world has ever seen,” Starmer told reporters during a press conference at Downing Street. “It has kept us safe for many decades, and we are fully committed to NATO.”

Starmer refused to bow to “pressure” from Trump, reaffirming the U.K.’s stance against wider involvement in the joint U.S.-Israeli military operation.    

“Whatever the pressure on me and others, whatever the noise, I’m going to act in the British national interest in all the decisions that I make,” he added. “That’s why I’ve been absolutely clear that this is not our war and we’re not going to get dragged into it.”

The prime minister’s defense comes as Trump has grown increasingly frustrated with European allies over their reluctance to deploy military assets to help secure the Strait of Hormuz, a critical maritime chokepoint that transports about one-fifth of the world’s oil supply.

The president told The Telegraph in an interview published early Wednesday that U.S. membership in NATO was “beyond reconsideration.”

“I was never swayed by NATO. I always knew they were a paper tiger, and [Russian President Vladimir] Putin knows that too, by the way,” Trump said, echoing comments made by Secretary of State Marco Rubio a day earlier.

The president singled out the U.K. and Starmer in his rebuke — as he’s done repeatedly since the Middle East conflict began more than a month ago — by suggesting the Royal Navy was not up for the task of policing the strait.  

“You don’t even have a navy. You’re too old and had aircraft carriers that didn’t work,” he said, adding he wasn’t “going to tell [Starmer] what to do” on defense spending.

“He can do whatever he wants. It doesn’t matter,” Trump told the outlet. “All Starmer wants is costly windmills that are driving your energy prices through the roof.”

Starmer blocked a request in late February for U.S. planes to use U.K. bases for preemptive strikes on Tehran over concerns it could violate international law. He later reversed course as Tehran escalated its retaliatory campaign in the region, allowing the use of British bases to carry out strikes against Iranian missile sites being used to target vessels.

The U.K. has also been bolstering its military assets in the region, deploying additional troops and air defense systems to join Typhoon jets already stationed in Qatar.

Facing strained U.S. relations, Starmer declared on Wednesday that the U.K. is pursuing closer economic and security cooperation with Europe.

“It is increasingly clear that as the world continues down this volatile path, our long-term national interest requires closer partnership with our allies in Europe and with the European Union,” he said.

data.title: Trump: Miami presidential library will 'most likely' have hotel
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday said his Miami presidential library will “most likely” have a hotel attached. 

“It’s gonna be most likely a hotel, you know? This concept could be office, but it’s most likely gonna be a hotel with a beautiful building underneath and a 747 Air Force One in the lobby,” the president told reporters in the Oval Office.

He said the project would be completed on time, month after ripping former President Obama for being behind on the construction of his presidential library in Chicago.

“I think you’re gonna see a great one here and it’ll go up on time, on budget, best location in Miami,” Trump told reporters.

“They say it’s the best block in Miami and the state worked with us,” he added.

Trump’s son, Eric, is helping with the new real estate development on a property donated by Florida and Miami Dade College. 

Eric Trump recently told the Miami Herald there would be no apartments or condominiums in the building and also shared renderings of the proposed presidential library on the social platform X.

“Over the past six months, I have poured my heart and soul into this project with my incredible team at @Trump,” Eric Trump wrote in a Monday post on X.

“This landmark on the water in Miami, Florida will stand as a lasting testament to an amazing man, an amazing developer, and the greatest President our Nation has ever known. These images have never been seen by the public — until today. Enjoy,” he added.

Eric Trump serves as executive vice president of the family’s real estate company, the Trump Organization, and frequently helps develop new projects tied to their surname. 

He played a key role in securing the approval of a $3 billion condo development at the Trump National Doral resort in Miami last year. 

The family is also planning to add a casino to the property, according to reports.

data.title: Trump draws applause at Kennedy Center opening of 'Chicago'
data.description:
President Trump received a mostly positive reception during an appearance at the Kennedy Center on Tuesday evening, as he and first lady Melania Trump attended the opening of the musical “Chicago.”

As the first couple entered, those in the crowd who booed were drowned out by louder cheers, according to The Associated Press. It marked their first trip to the performing arts center since the first lady’s eponymous documentary film premiered there in January.

“Chicago,” which is set in the 1920s, is the longest-running American musical on Broadway. It tells the story of Roxie Hart, a nightclub dancer who murders her lover and tries to avoid going to jail. The musical will run at the Kennedy Center Opera House through this Sunday.

Since returning to office, Trump has exerted his influence over the Kennedy Center, which opened in 1971 as a memorial to former President Kennedy. Trump hand-picked new members of the board last year and anointed himself as chair.

In December, the new board voted to add Trump’s name to the building, a move that congressional Democrats said could not occur without legislation. That marked just one of many instances in which the Trump administration has added the president’s name to buildings and programs, including the U.S. Institute for Peace and the TrumpRx prescription drug platform. 

After a July 4 celebration, the center is scheduled to close for renovations for two years — a closure pushed by Trump, who argued that the building needed maintenance. But multiple legal challenges have sought to halt the renovation, including one from Rep. Joyce Beatty (D-Ohio), an ex officio member of the center’s board. 

The week before the center closes, comedian Bill Maher will receive this year’s Mark Twain Prize for American Humor on June 28. White House officials initially denied reports earlier this month that the HBO “Real Time” host, a frequent critic of Trump who dined with the president last year, would receive the award.

Previous recipients of the award include Whoopi Goldberg, Steve Martin, Billy Crystal, Tina Fey and David Letterman, among other notable comedians.

data.title: Trump says US will be 'out of Iran pretty quickly,' could return for 'spot hits'
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday said the U.S. will be “out of Iran pretty quickly” after nearly five weeks of airstrikes on the Middle Eastern country but warned that it could come back for some “spot hits.”

Trump made the remarks in a phone interview with Reuters when asked if the U.S. could consider the war with Iran over. The president has offered varying timelines, one point earlier this month saying he will know the conflict is over when he can “feel it in my bones.“

“I can’t tell you exactly … we’re going to be out pretty quickly,” he said, later adding that the Iranian regime, “won’t have a nuclear weapon because they are incapable of that now, and then I’ll leave, and I’ll take everybody with me, and if we have to we’ll come back to do spot hits.”

Trump claimed that Iran has “had full regime change” following the death of former supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on the first day of strikes. His son, Mojtaba Khamenei, was tapped to be the new leader early last month.

“I’m dealing with a very good chance that we’ll make a deal because they don’t want to be blasted anymore,” Trump told Reuters. “I didn’t need regime change, but we got it because of the casualties of war.”

“We got it. So, we have regime change and the big thing we have ⁠is they’re ​not going to have a nuclear weapon,” he continued. “Nor do they want one.”

The president said Operation Epic Fury has ensured that Iran will not have a nuclear weapon. The Islamic Republic has long denied that it has been developing a nuclear weapon, having previously said its nuclear program is meant for peaceful means.

Trump told the outlet that he does not care about Iran’s highly enriched uranium. The Defense Department has considered sending troops to the region to extract the country’s uranium. But he told Reuters that it’s “so far underground.”

“We’ll always be watching it by satellite,” Trump said.

Earlier on Wednesday, he announced that Iran asked for a ceasefire.

The president wrote on Truth Social that he would consider the country’s proposal once the Strait of Hormuz is “open, free, and clear” and praised Iran’s “New Regime President.” It is unclear if he was referring to the younger Khamenei, as he did not reveal who the leader was.

“We will consider when Hormuz Strait is open, free, and clear,” Trump added in the post. “Until then, we are blasting Iran into oblivion or, as they say, back to the Stone Ages!!!”

The president is expected to address the nation later Wednesday outlining the ongoing U.S. military operation. Few details have been revealed, but White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said the speech will provide an “important update on Iran.”

data.title: Afroman's verdict is just the beginning: Officers also deserve criminal scrutiny
data.description:
On March 18, a jury in rural Adams County, Ohio, rejected a defamation lawsuit in a matter of hours. That speed was itself a verdict — not just on the merits, but on the character of the case.

Seven law enforcement officers had sued a music artist for using his own home security footage to criticize a police raid on his own home. The officers lost, in what civil liberties advocates called a huge First Amendment victory.

The more important question, though, is going largely unasked — whether the officers who brought this lawsuit should face criminal scrutiny as well.

In August 2022, deputies from the county sheriff’s Department executed a search warrant at the home of Joseph Foreman, known professionally as Afroman. They entered with force, damaged property and seized cash on suspicion of drug trafficking and kidnapping. No charges followed. The money was eventually returned.

Foreman had security cameras, so he used the footage in music videos and social media posts that mocked the deputies by name and face. The videos spread widely. Some generated commercial revenue.

The deputies’ response was a $3.9 million lawsuit alleging defamation, invasion of privacy and misappropriation of their likenesses.

The ACLU described the lawsuit as “nothing short of absurd.” The more precise term is “strategic.” This is the structure of what courts call a SLAPP — a Strategic Lawsuit Against Public Participation — which attempts to impose legal costs and reputational risk upon speakers until they give up, deciding the speech is not worth it.

At trial, the deputies argued that Foreman’s criticism had made their jobs more dangerous — that a citizen’s use of his own footage constituted a threat to officer safety. The jury quickly rejected that idea. What it could not do was determine whether it was itself a constitutional violation for someone backed by state authority to bring such an argument.

Every American law enforcement officer swears an oath to uphold the Constitution. In practice, it is a promise that gets repeated and seldom enforced. The officers who brought this lawsuit were not rookies confused about the boundaries of protected speech. They were experienced state actors who understood or were obligated to understand that a citizen’s criticism of their official conduct — using footage legally recorded on his own property — sits near the center of First Amendment protection. 

The oath is relevant not because it proves intent, but because it makes a claim of ignorance implausible. These officers were people who exercised state authority against speech they disliked, despite having sworn not to do exactly that.

Federal law under Title 18 Section 242 prohibits willful deprivation of constitutional rights by someone acting under color of law. Courts have interpreted “willful” to mean that the defendant was aware that his or her conduct was unlawful — not merely that the conduct turned out to be unlawful in hindsight.

In this case, state actors, not private citizens, brought the lawsuit. The target of the suit was speech specifically, criticism of those same actors’ official conduct. The speech involved legally obtained footage recorded inside the speaker’s home. The lawsuit sought $3.9 million and spanned years of litigation. And the officers were bound by oath to the constitutional limits they arguably worked to circumvent.

Prosecutors owe Afroman a genuine investigation into this case.

These officers executed a search warrant, made no arrests, damaged and seized property and then spent years litigating against the homeowner for criticizing them. If they can do all that and face no consequences beyond losing a civil lawsuit they themselves brought, then the message is not that the system works. The message is that it tolerates a particular kind of abuse, provided that abuse arrives in the form of a court filing rather than a baton.

The next person in Afroman’s position may not have his platform or the resilience to fight back. And if the only deterrent against this kind of lawsuit is the chance that the target can afford to win, then the deterrent is not a principle, it is a lottery.

Criminal scrutiny would not criminalize litigation. It would signal that litigation, like every other instrument of state power, has constitutional limits and that those limits apply to the people who swore to uphold them.

What no jury was asked, and what no prosecutor has yet seriously engaged, is whether the filing of this lawsuit, by these officers, against this speech, constituted a willful abuse of state authority under federal law. When officers swear to uphold constitutional rights and then use the machinery of litigation to suppress them, the oath is not merely broken. It is inverted.

The jury did its part. Now the question is whether prosecutors will do theirs.

Michael Mellette is a philosopher and writer. Follow his work at: michaelmellette.substack.com.

data.title: Why voters perceive Trump as 'awesome' — and what Democrats can learn from it
data.description:
Recently, I came across a report that did a better job explaining President Trump’s unique connection to certain voters than anything I have encountered since he first came to power. Yet it never even mentions the president’s name. In fact, it was published 12 years before Donald Trump first declared for the Republican nomination.

I’m talking about a 2003 paper written by Jonathan Haidt and Dacher Keltner for the journal Cognition and Emotion: “Approaching awe, a moral, spiritual, and aesthetic emotion.” By explaining the psychology behind awe, it helps answer the most frequently asked question in American politics: How does Trump — a billionaire who brazenly exploits his presidency to pad his net worth, adorns the Oval Office in gold, and affixes his name to the marble facades of government buildings — manage to connect with so many voters struggling in the shadows? How does he appeal so successfully to lower-income voters when he raises these voters’ bills through inflationary tariffs and expensive wars?

You may think the answer is simple, but it goes deep — to the areas of the brain that activate emotion. It turns out that many voters experience the same neurophysiological response that we get when we observe a majestic landscape, a feat of athletic prowess, or a spiritual awakening.

He triggers awe.

Haidt and Keltner’s paper is a relatively early contribution to the study of awe as an emotion — a field of research that has proven especially relevant in today’s politics. (Full disclosure, I have been studying awe recently as chairman of the Birch Creek Dialogues, which explores how we can leverage the power of awe to enhance civic engagement.) In a nation divided by ideology, the intangible quality of a candidate’s ability to command awe may be a greater factor in winning elections than we previously realized.

In Haidt and Keltner’s framework, there are two criteria that must be satisfied to induce awe as an emotion. First, something so vast — bigger than ourselves — that it diminishes our own self-perception. Second, an experience that challenges or negates our mental structures as we accommodate to make sense of it. When combined, the experiences can trigger a neurophysiological response that widens and glistens your eyes, lifts your head, sends shivers across your skin, reshapes your mouth to emit a breathless, “Whoa!”

How does Trump fit into that framework? Are we supposed to consider him in the same light as the awe induced by seeing the Grand Canyon, the aurora borealis, or Albert Bierstadt’s painting “Among the Sierra Nevada, California” in the Smithsonian? Well, Trump’s awe is different, but it is equally powerful. It stems from “primordial awe.”

Haidt and Keltner built on the work of others to propose that the primordial form of awe is the individual’s feeling toward a vastly more powerful other. In simple terms, it stems, in part, from our tribal origins, when survival required cohesion and cohesion demanded hierarchical leadership. “This feeling is likely to involve reverence, devotion, and the inclination to subordinate one’s own interests and goals in deference to those of the powerful leader,” they write.

When Trump wields his bombastic rhetoric from his gold-adorned Oval Office; as cranes construct his new ballroom nearby; as he commands masked and armed paramilitary forces that sweep into our cities, he cultivates a sense of awe in his supporters that reinforces his perch at the top of the hierarchy of American politics.

Trump’s awe has proven so powerful because his arrival to the political scene coincided with large swaths of the electorate feeling vulnerable. They were threatened by a unique convergence of events: 9/11, the 2008 economic meltdown, the implosion of previously trusted institutions (scandals that rocked sports, churches and banks), the rise of social media, the technological sea-change of automation and artificial intelligence, the pressures of increasing migration and globalization, and a devastating global pandemic.

When we feel most vulnerable, we turn to leaders who represent strength and authority: Leaders who will protect us (or our stock portfolios) invoke a sense of awe.

Trump isn’t the only politician who’s been propelled to office by inducing awe among supporters. President Barack Obama was the last Democrat on the national stage who managed to project awe, with his commanding oratory and charismatic presence. Elsewhere, in international politics, look to larger-than-life figures such as Giorgia Meloni, who is poised to become the longest continuously serving post-war Italian prime minister, and Volodymyr Zelensky, whose powerful voice has helped steer Ukraine through the crisis provoked by Russia’s unlawful invasion.

As Democrats try to define what voters think about Trump’s presidency, we cannot make the mistake of reducing voters’ attitudes to a discrete set of poll-tested policy leanings. Campaigns are about polls and points and data; but it’s often the secret sauce that wins. What counts is not how voters think about Trump’s ideological positioning — or that of Vice President JD Vance, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, or whoever else might emerge as Trump’s MAGA successor — it is what emotions are motivating their behavior. 

Yes, a leader’s policies still do matter. There are, of course, countless variables that go into winning a national election. But in this era of politics, one thing is clear: The candidate that inspires awe in voters is the one that can win awesome victories at the ballot box.

Steve Israel represented New York in the House of Representatives for eight terms and was chairman of the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee from 2011 to 2015.

data.title: Mark Kelly on Artemis II mission: 'This is something we've never done before'
data.description:
Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.), a retired NASA astronaut, said Wednesday that the Artemis II mission to the moon is uncharted territory for American space explorers. 

“They’re going to go 4,000 miles on the far side of the moon, which is something we’ve never done before, even during Apollo,” Kelly told NewsNation’s Henry Amor at Kennedy Center Space Center in Florida, while donning his NASA uniform and a camo Navy hat. 

“The Apollo crews went in low moon, low lunar orbit,” he added. “So this is something we’ve never done before.”

Kelly and his identical twin, Scott Kelly, are the first and only siblings to have traveled into orbit — although they never did it together. Mark Kelly, who retired from NASA in 2011, has spent more than 50 days in space and traveled more than 20 million miles, according to his Senate website. 

The Artemis II test flight is scheduled for 6:24 p.m. ET on Wednesday evening, a trip that will mark the first crewed mission to head toward the moon since the final Apollo flight in December 1972. It will follow the uncrewed Artemis I moon-orbiting mission in 2022. 

The Arizona senator said that while he is “incredibly excited” for the mission, he “can’t imagine” he is “nearly as excited” as crew members Victor Glover, Jeremy Hansen, Christina Koch and Reid Wiseman.

Koch, an astronaut since 2013, said during a Sunday news conference that “things are certainly starting to feel real,” according to The New York Times. Ahead of the mission, Koch and her fellow crew members are in quarantine under “strict health monitoring,” and are completing medical checks to “ensure fitness for launch,” according to a Monday release from NASA.

The 10-day mission will take the astronauts around the moon, during which NASA will test the Orion spacecraft’s life support systems for the first time with people and “lay the groundwork” for future crewed Artemis missions, NASA said in another Monday release.

“It’s a new kind of mission. I’m excited about it because this is, essentially, one of the steps we need to put U.S. astronauts back onto the surface of the moon,” Kelly remarked.

Prelaunch coverage is already underway on NASA’s YouTube channel. Full coverage will start at 12:50 p.m. ET on NASA+, the agency’s free streaming service. Coverage will continue on YouTube after Orion’s solar array wings deploy in space.

data.title: JetBlue raises checked baggage fees amid soaring fuel prices
data.description:
TAMPA, Fla. (WFLA) – JetBlue is raising its checked baggage fees, citing increased operational costs.

The move also comes amid rising economic uncertainty and spiking oil prices in the month since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran.

“As we experience rising operating costs, we regularly evaluate how to manage those costs while keeping base fares competitive and continuing to invest in the experience our customers value,” JetBlue said in a statement.

The airline still allows passengers to bring on a free carry-on bag and a personal item. If they want to check a bag, the price will depend on whether or not they’re traveling during “peak travel dates.”

The following dates are considered “peak” times, according to JetBlue:

  • April 2026: 4/2-4/27
  • May 2026: 5/21-5/25
  • June 2026: 6/11-6/30
  • July 2026: 7/1-7/31
  • August 2026: 8/1-8/31
  • September 2026: 9/1-9/9
  • October 2026: 10/10-10/14
  • November 2026: 11/19-11/30
  • December 2026: 12/17-12/31
  • January 2027: 1/1-1/18
  • February 2027: 2/11-2/22
  • April 2027: 4/1-4/26
  • May 2027: 5/20-5/24

On those dates, travelers will pay $49 to check a bag, up from $40. During non-peak times, the cost of checking a bag is $39, up from $35.

If paying to check a bag less than 24 hours before departure, customers will also be charged an additional $10 fee.

“Adjusting fees for optional services used by select customers, such as checked baggage, allows us to continue offering more competitive fares while delivering the onboard experience our customers love, including complimentary snacks and drinks, unlimited, high-speed Wi-Fi and seatback entertainment screens,” JetBlue’s statement read. “While we recognize that fee increases are never ideal, we take careful consideration to ensure these changes are implemented only when necessary.”

There are some exemptions to the checked baggage fee, such as customers with JetBlue credit cards or those with elite status in the airline’s loyalty program.

More information about JetBlue’s checked bag policy can be found on the airline’s website.

data.title: Live updates: Trump upends tradition, attends Supreme Court arguments on birthright citizenship
data.description:
The Supreme Court on Wednesday is weighing President Trump’s executive order seeking to end birthright citizenship.

The president is in the courtroom, meaning he is believed to be the first sitting president to attend oral arguments. At least twice this week he has ripped into the 14th Amendment’s citizenship guarantee.

At 9 p.m. EDT, Trump will address the nation from the White House on the Iran conflict. On Tuesday, he said he expected that U.S. troops would exit the region in about “two weeks.”

On Capitol Hill, the Senate’s bill to fund the Department of Homeland Security is gaining favor with the president.

Meanwhile, a push to end the ongoing U.S. military operation in Iran is gaining steam with House lawmakers.

Trump on Wednesday will attend an Easter lunch after oral arguments.

Follow live below.
